original_abstract: Since the inception of the Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) initiative in Afghanistan, members of the nongovernmental organization (NGO) community have voiced significant concerns, arguing that the integration of military and humanitarian operations creates "blurred lines" that prevent insurgents from distinguishing between combatants and aid workers. Many organizations have hypothesized that this overlap directly increases the likelihood of attacks on humanitarian personnel. Despite the prevalence of this claim in policy circles and media outlets, there remains a notable lack of empirical evidence to validate it. This study addresses this gap by employing a panel-corrected standard error regression model to analyze data across all 34 Afghan provinces during 2010 and 2011. While preliminary results indicate that NGOs reported a higher frequency of security incidents in provinces containing PRTs, a more nuanced analysis reveals that this correlation was exclusive to provinces where teams were led by non-U.S. forces. These findings challenge the universality of the "blurred lines" theory as a comprehensive explanation for the deterioration of aid worker security.
humanized_abstract: Since the deployment of Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) personnel to Afghanistan, NGOs have voiced a number of concerns related to the blending of military and humanitarian aid activities. Chief among these is the assertion that the activities conducted by the PRTs and the US military have obviated any distinction between combatants and humanitarian aid workers, thereby increasing the likelihood of attack by insurgents. Though widely referenced in policy and media statements, this assertion has not been subject to rigorous empirical scrutiny. The purpose of this paper is to empirically investigate the impact of PRT deployments on the environment in which aid workers operate. Using a panel-corrected standard error regression model applied to data across all 34 provinces of Afghanistan for calendar years 2010 and 2011, the study concludes that the number of incidents reported by NGOs were higher in provinces with a PRT presence. However, a detailed examination of the coefficients reveals that these incidents were largely confined to those provinces that were commanded by foreign forces (i.e. not US troops). Hence, the commonly referenced "blurred lines" assertion, which has been used to explain increased attacks against aid workers, does not universally apply.
